writing_type: personal food and travel blog posts
user_goals_summary: Writes immersive, first-person food and travel narratives that prioritize embodied sensory experience over observation. Values warm conversational voice with self-deprecating humor, and seeks small, hyper-specific details that carry outsized emotional weight — making the reader feel they are inside the experience rather than reading about it.

rubric.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Sensory descriptions should feel physical and pre-rational — acting on the reader's body rather than registering as observations. Writing engages multiple senses (not just sight), and the reader should feel they are inside the moment, not looking at a picture of it.

dimensions.label: Engages at least three senses (not relying on visual description alone)
Sensory details act on the body — smells pull, heat hits, sounds interrupt — rather than being passively observed
Physical or instinctive responses are described before intellectual processing or narrative reflection
Spaces and environments are conveyed through how they feel to inhabit, not just how they look

rubric.name: Specificity Over Convention
rubric.description: Descriptions must feel particular to this exact experience, resisting category-level language that could apply to any version of the thing. If a descriptor could be swapped into a generic example of the same subject, it isn't doing enough work.

dimensions.label: Avoids adjectives or phrases that merely describe the general category (e.g., 'rich broth' for any tonkotsu)
Includes at least one detail so specific it could only belong to this particular experience
Metaphors and comparisons illuminate something unexpected rather than confirming what the reader already assumes
Key descriptive details serve more than one narrative purpose (e.g., atmosphere and character, or transition and history)

rubric.name: Conversational Voice & Self-Awareness
rubric.description: The writing should feel like telling a friend a story — warm, unguarded, and lightly self-deprecating. The narrator is aware of their own ridiculousness and willing to undercut romantic moments with honesty, without losing sincerity.

dimensions.label: Tone reads as spoken to a friend, not performed for an audience
Includes at least one moment of honest self-awareness or light humor at the narrator's own expense
Humor and self-awareness never undermine genuine emotional moments
Parenthetical thoughts, qualifiers, or casual asides feel natural rather than stylistically forced

rubric.name: Pacing & Transitional Craft
rubric.description: Key moments are given space to breathe — white space, paragraph breaks, and unhurried sentences signal what matters. Transitions between spaces, moods, or time feel seamless and earned, pulling the reader forward without announcing the shift.

dimensions.label: The most important moments occupy their own space (paragraph or pause) rather than being compressed alongside lesser details
The reader moves through the narrative without noticing structural transitions — arrival feels natural, not signposted
Shifts between scenes or spaces are anchored in a sensory change (temperature, sound, smell) rather than narrated logistically
Each section builds on the previous one so the reader feels momentum rather than a sequence of disconnected descriptions

rubric.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
rubric.description: The emotional core of the piece lives in small, precise details — a gesture, a texture, a sound — not in grand declarations of feeling. These moments should land with quiet force, carrying more weight than their literal scale suggests.

dimensions.label: At least one tiny, specific detail delivers a disproportionately large emotional impact
Emotion is conveyed through observed detail and action, not through the narrator stating how they felt
Emotionally charged moments use restraint and precision rather than piling on adjectives or intensifiers
People are revealed through small physical actions or habits rather than direct description of personality

coaching_notes: This user diagnoses their own drafts with unusual precision — they can name exactly what's wrong and often articulate the *quality* they want even when they can't produce the specific language. Offer multiple options when revising a key passage; they choose well when given concrete alternatives. Never sacrifice sensory specificity or embodied physicality for efficiency — this user will always choose a longer, more immersive passage over a tighter but more observational one. Understatement is strongly preferred over atmosphere-loading in emotional moments.

rubric.description: Descriptions must feel particular to this exact experience, resisting category-level language that could apply to any version of the thing. If a descriptor could be swapped into a generic example of the same subject, it isn't doing enough work.

dimensions.label: Avoids adjectives or phrases that merely describe the general category (e.g., 'rich broth' for any tonkotsu)
Includes at least one detail so specific it could only belong to this particular experience

Key descriptive details serve more than one narrative purpose (e.g., atmosphere and character, or transition and history)

writing_type: personal food and travel blog posts
user_goals_summary: Writes immersive, first-person food and travel narratives that prioritize embodied sensory experience over observation. Values warm conversational voice with self-deprecating humor, and seeks small, hyper-specific details that carry outsized emotional weight — making the reader feel they are inside the experience rather than reading about it.
coaching_notes: This user diagnoses their own drafts with unusual precision — they can name exactly what's wrong and often articulate the *quality* they want even when they can't produce the specific language. Offer multiple options when revising a key passage; they choose well when given concrete alternatives. Never sacrifice sensory specificity or embodied physicality for efficiency — this user will always choose a longer, more immersive passage over a tighter but more observational one. Understatement is strongly preferred over atmosphere-loading in emotional moments.

rubric.name: Specificity Over Convention
rubric.description: Descriptions must feel particular to this exact experience, resisting category-level language that could apply to any version of the thing. If a descriptor could be swapped into a generic example of the same subject, it isn't doing enough work. The best details make the reader feel more inside the experience — comparisons that open up unexpected angles, physical sensations that haven't been pre-packaged, anything that breaks the expected pattern without calling attention to itself.

dimensions.label: Avoids adjectives or phrases that merely describe the general category (e.g., 'rich broth' for any tonkotsu, 'bustling streets' for any city market)
Includes at least one detail so specific it could only belong to this particular experience

Metaphors and comparisons illuminate something unexpected rather than confirming what the reader already assumes — the best comparisons feel found rather than engineered. If they happen to cross into unexpected territory (non-food, non-sensory), that's a sign they're working, not a goal in itself. Note: a slightly familiar phrase that lands cleanly can outperform a technically novel one that calls attention to itself — familiarity isn't automatically disqualifying.
An unexpected comparison that interrupts the reader's flow or feels visibly constructed has overcorrected — the test is whether it lands cleanly, not whether it surprises
Key descriptive details serve more than one narrative purpose (e.g., atmosphere and character, or transition and history)
No generic travel cliches or stock phrases that could describe any destination (e.g., 'hidden gem,' 'locals and tourists alike,' 'a feast for the senses')

rubric.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Every scene must include at least one non-visual sensory detail — sound, smell, temperature, texture. Visual-only descriptions read like looking at photos rather than being there. Sensory writing should feel physical and pre-rational, acting on the reader's body rather than registering as observations.

dimensions.label: Every scene includes at least one non-visual sensory detail (sound, smell, temperature, or texture) — visual-only is a failure condition
Across the piece, at least three senses are engaged
Sensory details act on the body — smells pull, heat hits, sounds interrupt — rather than being passively observed
Spaces and environments are conveyed through how they feel to inhabit, not just how they look

rubric.name: Anecdotes That Pull Their Weight
rubric.description: Personal anecdotes should do two things at once: make the reader feel present in the moment AND set up or connect to the next point about food, place, or culture. An anecdote that's only about the narrator — without connecting outward — feels self-indulgent. Every story earns its place by doing narrative work beyond just being charming.

dimensions.label: Each personal anecdote creates a sense of presence (the reader feels there) AND transitions into or illuminates a point about the food, place, or culture
No anecdote exists purely for self-expression — every personal story connects outward to the larger subject
Anecdotes feel woven in naturally, not dropped in as asides or interruptions to the main thread

rubric.name: Immersion-Then-Context Pacing
rubric.description: The writing should move rhythmically between being IN the experience and understanding it. Drop the reader into a scene first — sensory, immediate, present — then pull back briefly to ground the moment (how you got there, why it matters). This in-out rhythm is what separates travel writing from either a diary entry or a guidebook.

dimensions.label: Scenes open with immersion (sensory, immediate) before pulling back to provide context or reflection
Context and background are provided AFTER the reader is already inside the experience, not before
The most important moments occupy their own space (paragraph or pause) rather than being compressed alongside lesser details
The reader moves through the narrative without noticing structural transitions — arrival feels natural, not signposted
Each section builds on the previous one so the reader feels momentum rather than a sequence of disconnected descriptions

rubric.name: Conversational Voice & Honest Humor
rubric.description: The writing should feel like telling a friend a story — warm, unguarded, and occasionally self-deprecating. Humor should emerge from honest observation or self-awareness, never from exaggeration or trying to be clever. The narrator is aware of their own ridiculousness without performing it.

dimensions.label: Tone reads as spoken to a friend, not performed for an audience
Any humor comes from honest observation or self-awareness — not exaggeration, wordplay, or trying to land a joke
Humor and self-awareness never undermine genuine emotional moments
Parenthetical thoughts, qualifiers, or casual asides feel natural rather than stylistically forced
No overly promotional language — enthusiasm is earned through specificity, not superlatives

rubric.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
rubric.description: The emotional core of the piece lives in small, precise details — a gesture, a texture, a sound — not in grand declarations of feeling. These moments should land with quiet force, carrying more weight than their literal scale suggests.

dimensions.label: At least one tiny, specific detail delivers a disproportionately large emotional impact
Emotion is conveyed through observed detail and action, not through the narrator stating how they felt
Emotionally charged moments use restraint and precision rather than piling on adjectives or intensifiers
People are revealed through small physical actions or habits rather than direct description of personality

rubric.description: Personal anecdotes should do two things at once: make the reader feel present in the moment AND connect outward to something about food, place, or culture. An anecdote that's only about the narrator feels self-indulgent. Every story earns its place by doing narrative work beyond just being charming.

dimensions.label: Each personal anecdote creates a sense of presence AND transitions into or illuminates a point about the food, place, or culture
No anecdote exists purely for self-expression — every personal story connects outward to the larger subject
Anecdotes feel woven in naturally, not dropped in as asides or interruptions to the main thread
When an anecdote closes a section, it arrives as the destination the whole piece was heading toward — not tacked on but inevitable

rubric.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
rubric.description: The emotional core of the piece lives in small, precise details — a gesture, a sound, a texture — not in grand declarations of feeling. These moments should land with quiet force, carrying more weight than their literal scale suggests.

dimensions.label: At least one tiny, specific detail delivers a disproportionately large emotional impact
Emotion is conveyed through observed detail and action, not through the narrator stating how they felt
Emotionally charged moments use restraint and precision rather than piling on adjectives or intensifiers
People are revealed through small physical actions or habits rather than direct description of personality

rubric.name: Conversational Voice & Honest Humor
rubric.description: The writing should feel like telling a friend a story — warm, unguarded, and occasionally self-deprecating. Humor emerges from honest observation or self-awareness, never from exaggeration or trying to be clever. The personal quality bar is whether key lines reward reading aloud.

dimensions.label: Tone reads as spoken to a friend, not performed for an audience
Humor comes from honest observation or self-awareness — not exaggeration, wordplay, or trying to land a joke
Humor and self-awareness never undermine genuine emotional moments
Key lines have a rhythm and cadence that reward reading aloud — the 'would I read this back to myself and feel something' test

user_goals_summary: Writes immersive, sensory-first food and travel pieces that make the reader feel physically present in the moment. Values extreme specificity over generic descriptions, pre-rational body responses over intellectual observations, and a warm conversational voice with honest humor. The personal quality bar is whether a line rewards reading aloud.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: visual-only scene descriptions are a failure condition — caught and flagged in two of three iterations. This user's quality bar is the read-aloud test ('would I say this back to myself and feel smug about it'). When pushing for more unexpected metaphors, stay on the clean side of the line — a slightly familiar image that deepens the moment always beats a novel one that reads like a riddle or interrupts flow. Generic emotional closings are treated as severely as generic descriptions.

user_goals_summary: Writes first-person travel and food pieces that drop readers into embodied, multi-sensory moments before providing context. Values extreme specificity over convention, pre-rational body-first responses, and endings that stay inside experience rather than declaring insight. The personal quality bar is whether key lines reward reading aloud.

rubric.name: Specificity Over Convention
rubric.description: Descriptions must feel particular to this exact experience, resisting category-level language that could apply to any version of the thing. The best details make the reader feel more inside the experience — comparisons that open unexpected angles, physical sensations that haven't been pre-packaged, anything that breaks the expected pattern without calling attention to itself. This applies equally to closing beats, which must not revert to generic emotional shorthand.

dimensions.label: Avoids category-level descriptors, travel clichés, and stock phrases that could apply to any version of the subject (e.g., 'rich broth' for any tonkotsu, 'hidden gem,' 'a feast for the senses')
Includes at least one detail so specific it could only belong to this particular experience

Metaphors cross into unexpected territory but land cleanly — deepening the moment rather than pulling the reader out of it. A slightly familiar phrase that lands without friction outperforms a novel one that feels like a riddle
Key descriptive details serve more than one narrative purpose (e.g., atmosphere and character, or sensory transition and history of the place)
Closing beats and emotional landing points are as specific as opening descriptions — no generic 'I didn't want to leave' or 'it felt like home' sentiments that could close any cozy-place piece

rubric.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
rubric.description: Every scene must include at least one non-visual sensory detail — sound, smell, temperature, texture. Visual-only descriptions read like looking at photos rather than being there. Sensory writing should feel physical and pre-rational, acting on the reader's body rather than registering as observations. The body decides before the brain catches up.

dimensions.label: Every scene includes at least one non-visual sensory detail (sound, smell, temperature, or texture) — visual-only is a failure condition
Across the piece, at least three senses are engaged
Sensory details act on the body — smells pull, heat hits, sounds interrupt — rather than being passively observed
Physical and instinctive responses are conveyed before intellectual processing — the body decides before the brain catches up
Spaces and environments are conveyed through how they feel to inhabit, not just how they look

rubric.name: Immersion-Then-Context Pacing
rubric.description: The writing drops the reader into a scene first — sensory, immediate, present — then pulls back briefly to ground the moment. This in-out rhythm separates immersive travel writing from either a diary entry or a guidebook. The most important moments get space to breathe.

dimensions.label: Scenes open with sensory immersion before pulling back to provide context or reflection
The most important moments occupy their own space (paragraph or pause) rather than being compressed alongside lesser details
The reader moves through the narrative without noticing structural transitions — arrival feels natural, not signposted
Each section builds on the previous one so the reader feels momentum rather than a sequence of disconnected descriptions

rubric.name: Understanding Earned Through Experience
rubric.description: Insights and understanding should arrive through physical experience and action rather than being declared as reflection. The narrator stays inside the not-knowing — endings and realizations feel half-formed and embodied, not pre-packaged. A scene that 'clicks shut' with a stated lesson feels premature; one that stays in the doing feels authentic.

dimensions.label: The narrator does not arrive at understanding before the scene has earned it — no declared lessons that haven't been physically lived through
Sections can end in productive not-knowing — staying inside the experience is a legitimate and often stronger resolution than a clean takeaway
When understanding does arrive, it comes through hands, taste, smell, physical action — not through stepping back to reflect
Realizations feel like they are arriving to the narrator in the moment, not already formed and waiting to be revealed

rubric.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
rubric.description: The emotional core of the piece lives in small, precise details — a gesture, a sound, a texture — not in grand declarations of feeling. These moments should land with quiet force, carrying more weight than their literal scale suggests.

dimensions.label: At least one tiny, specific detail delivers a disproportionately large emotional impact
Emotion is conveyed through observed detail and action, not through the narrator stating how they felt
Emotionally charged moments use restraint and precision rather than piling on adjectives or intensifiers
People are revealed through small physical actions or habits rather than direct description of personality

rubric.name: Anecdotes That Pull Their Weight

An anecdote that's only about the narrator feels self-indulgent. Every story earns its place by doing narrative work beyond just being charming.

dimensions.label: Each personal anecdote creates a sense of presence AND transitions into or illuminates a point about the food, place, or culture
No anecdote exists purely for self-expression — every personal story connects outward to the larger subject
Anecdotes feel woven in naturally, not dropped in as asides or interruptions to the main thread
When an anecdote closes a section, it arrives as the destination the whole piece was heading toward — not tacked on but inevitable

rubric.name: Conversational Voice & Honest Humor
rubric.description: The writing should feel like telling a friend a story — warm, unguarded, and occasionally self-deprecating. Humor emerges from honest observation or self-awareness, never from exaggeration or trying to be clever. The personal quality bar is whether key lines reward reading aloud.

dimensions.label: Tone reads as spoken to a friend, not performed for an audience
Humor comes from honest observation or self-awareness — not exaggeration, wordplay, or trying to land a joke
Humor and self-awareness never undermine genuine emotional moments
Key lines have a rhythm and cadence that reward reading aloud — the 'would I read this back to myself and feel something' test

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every scene must feel inhabited, not photographed — visual-only description is treated as a failure condition. When metaphors risk feeling like riddles, pull back toward cleaner language rather than pushing further into novelty; this user consistently prefers a clean unexpected landing over a clever constructed one. Endings are the highest-sensitivity zone: generic emotional resolution ('I didn't want to leave') is rejected instantly, and premature insight ('here's what I learned') is rejected almost as fast. The ideal ending stays inside the experience — confused, embodied, still in motion.

This applies equally to closing beats, which must not revert to generic emotional shorthand.
